long_reaction_chains.name: Long Reaction Chains
long_reaction_chains.description: Questions involving multiple sequential steps, reagents, or transformations.

structural_patterns: Product of one step becomes reactant of next

counting_enumeration.name: Counting/Enumeration
counting_enumeration.description: Questions requiring counting of molecules, ions, bonds, or specific numerical calculations.

deep_mechanistic_reasoning.name: Deep Mechanistic Reasoning
deep_mechanistic_reasoning.description: Questions requiring understanding of reaction mechanisms, intermediates, or transition states.

comparative_ranking.name: Comparative Ranking
comparative_ranking.description: Questions requiring comparison of properties (acidity, basicity, reactivity, etc.) and arranging in orders.

questions.question_text: The equilibrium Cr2O7^2- ⇌ 2CrO4^2- (A) exists in basic medium (B) exists in acidic medium (C) exists in neutral medium (D) does not exist
questions.solution_summary: The equilibrium Cr2O7^2- + 2OH- ⇌ 2CrO4^2- + H2O exists in basic medium. In acidic medium, the reverse is favoured.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires knowledge of the chromate-dichromate equilibrium and its pH dependence.

questions.question_text: Which of the following is the strongest acid? (A) p-Cl-C6H4COOH (B) p-OH-C6H4COOH (C) C6H5COOH (D) p-NO2-C6H4COOH
questions.topic: Organic Chemistry - Aldehydes, Ketones and Carboxylic Acids
questions.sub_topic: Effect of substituents on acidity of benzoic acid
questions.solution_summary: Electron-withdrawing groups (like -NO2) increase acidity by stabilising the carboxylate ion via resonance and inductive effect. -NO2 at para position is strongly electron withdrawing, making p-nitrobenzoic acid the strongest acid.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires understanding of substituent effects (inductive and resonance) on carboxylic acid acidity.

questions.question_text: Maltose is made up of: (A) glucose and fructose (B) glucose and galactose (C) glucose and glucose (D) glucose and ribose
questions.solution_summary: Maltose is a disaccharide composed of two α-D-glucose units linked by a C1-C4 glycosidic linkage.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Pure recall of the composition of maltose.

questions.question_text: The system that forms maximum boiling azeotrope is: (A) CS2 and acetone (B) Benzene and toluene (C) Ethanol and acetone (D) Chloroform and acetone
questions.solution_summary: Chloroform and acetone show negative deviation from Raoult's law due to H-bonding between them, forming a maximum boiling azeotrope.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Knowledge of azeotropes and the systems that show positive/negative deviation.

questions.question_text: A zero-order reaction is one whose rate is independent of: (A) Presence of light (B) Concentration of the reactant (C) Temperature of the reaction (D) Pressure of the reaction
questions.solution_summary: For zero order reaction, rate = k, i.e., independent of concentration of reactants.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Definition of zero order reaction.

questions.question_text: In the Arrhenius equation, when log k is plotted against 1/T, a straight line is obtained whose: (A) slope is A/R and intercept is Ea (B) slope is A and intercept is -Ea/R (C) slope is -Ea/RT and intercept is log A (D) slope is -Ea/2.303R and intercept is log A
questions.solution_summary: log k = log A - (Ea/2.303R)(1/T). Hence slope = -Ea/2.303R and intercept = log A.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Recall of logarithmic form of Arrhenius equation and slope/intercept.

questions.question_text: Which of the following is most reactive towards nucleophilic substitution reaction? [Image: options: CH3-CH2-CH2-Cl, CH2=CH-CH2-Cl, etc.]
questions.solution_summary: Allylic halides (CH2=CH-CH2-Cl) are most reactive towards SN reactions due to resonance stabilisation of the carbocation intermediate (for SN1) or the transition state (for SN2).

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires comparing reactivity of alkyl, vinyl, and allylic halides based on carbocation stability and bond strength.

questions.question_text: The product (X) obtained in the following reaction is: [Image: reaction sequence]
questions.solution_summary: The reaction sequence involves oxidation/cyclisation leading to the product (X) as shown in option B.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires stepwise product prediction from given reactions.

questions.question_text: Which of the following has the highest boiling point? (A) CH3CHO (B) CH3COCl (C) CH3COCH3 (D) CH3COOH
questions.topic: Organic Chemistry - Aldehydes, Ketones and Carboxylic Acids
questions.solution_summary: Carboxylic acids have strong intermolecular hydrogen bonding (dimer formation), giving them higher boiling points than aldehydes, ketones, and acyl chlorides of similar molecular mass.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires comparison of intermolecular forces in different carbonyl compounds.

questions.question_text: Phenol does not undergo nucleophilic substitution reaction easily due to: (A) Partial double bond character of C-OH bond (B) Partial double bond character of C-C bond (C) Acidic nature of phenol (D) Instability of phenoxide ion
questions.topic: Organic Chemistry - Alcohols, Phenols and Ethers
questions.solution_summary: In phenol, the C-OH bond has partial double bond character due to resonance, making it stronger and less susceptible to nucleophilic substitution.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Understanding of resonance effect on C-O bond in phenol making it resistant to substitution.

questions.question_text: The suitable Grignard reagent used for the synthesis of CH3-CH(CH3)-CH2OH with methanol is: (A) (CH3)2CH-CH2MgBr/H2O (B) CH3MgBr/H2O (C) (CH3)2CHMgBr/H2O (D) CH3CH2CH2MgBr/H2O
questions.topic: Organic Chemistry - Alcohols, Phenols and Ethers
questions.sub_topic: Synthesis of alcohols using Grignard reagents
questions.solution_summary: 2-methylpropan-1-ol (CH3-CH(CH3)-CH2OH) can be prepared by reaction of formaldehyde (methanal) with (CH3)2CHMgBr followed by hydrolysis. The Grignard reagent adds to HCHO to give the primary alcohol after protonation.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires knowledge of Grignard reaction with aldehydes to form alcohols and identifying the correct reagent for the desired product.

questions.question_text: All proteins on hydrolysis give: (A) Enzymes (B) α-amino acids (C) Glucose (D) Polypeptides
questions.solution_summary: Proteins are polymers of α-amino acids. Complete hydrolysis yields α-amino acids.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Pure recall of products of protein hydrolysis.

questions.question_text: Assertion (A): Fluoroacetic acid is stronger acid than chloroacetic acid. Reason (R): This is due to greater electron withdrawing nature of F than Cl.
questions.topic: Organic Chemistry - Aldehydes, Ketones and Carboxylic Acids
questions.solution_summary: Both A and R are true and R correctly explains A. F is more electronegative than Cl, so it stabilises the carboxylate ion better, increasing acidity.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Understanding of inductive effect on acidity: F > Cl in electron withdrawal.

questions.question_text: Assertion (A): p-nitroaniline is a weaker base than p-toluidine. Reason (R): The electron donating -NO2 group in p-nitroaniline makes it a weaker base.
questions.solution_summary: Assertion is true: -NO2 is electron withdrawing, decreasing electron density on N, making it weaker base. Reason is false because -NO2 is electron withdrawing, not donating.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Effect of substituents on basicity: -NO2 is electron withdrawing, decreases basicity, not donating.

questions.question_text: Assertion (A): ΔmixH for an ideal solution is not equal to zero. Reason (R): A-B interactions in an ideal solution are same as between A-A and B-B interactions.
questions.solution_summary: Assertion is false: ΔmixH = 0 for ideal solution. Reason is true: A-B interactions are same as A-A and B-B. So assertion false, reason true.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Properties of ideal solutions: ΔmixH = 0, ΔmixV = 0.

questions.question_text: Assertion (A): Separation of Zr and Hf is difficult. Reason (R): Zr and Hf are radioactive.
questions.solution_summary: Assertion is true (Zr and Hf have similar sizes due to lanthanoid contraction). Reason is false: they are not radioactive; the difficulty is due to similar ionic radii.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Factual knowledge about lanthanoid contraction and its effect on Zr and Hf.

questions.question_text: What happens when D-glucose is treated with the following reagents? (a) H2N-OH (b) (CH3CO)2O
questions.correct_answer: (a) Glucose with hydroxylamine (H2NOH) gives glucose oxime. (b) Glucose with acetic anhydride (CH3CO)2O gives glucose pentaacetate.
questions.solution_summary: Glucose reacts with H2NOH to form oxime (addition of NH2OH to aldehyde group). With acetic anhydride, all five OH groups get acetylated to form pentaacetate.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Recall of characteristic reactions of glucose with hydroxylamine and acetylation.

questions.question_text: (a) Draw the structures of major monohalo products in each of the following reactions: [Image: reactions] OR (b) Give reasons for the following: (i) Grignard reagent should be prepared under anhydrous conditions. (ii) Alkyl halides give alcohol with aqueous KOH whereas in the presence of alcoholic KOH, alkenes are formed.
questions.topic: Organic Chemistry - Haloalkanes and Haloarenes / Alcohols
questions.correct_answer: Part (a) involves drawing products based on Markovnikov addition and free radical halogenation. Part (b)(i) Grignard reagents react with water; anhydrous conditions prevent decomposition. (b)(ii) Aqueous KOH gives substitution (SN2) to form alcohol; alcoholic KOH gives elimination to form alkene.
questions.solution_summary: Products of halogenation and reasons for anhydrous conditions and solvent-dependent products.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Understanding of SN2 vs E2, and Grignard reagent sensitivity to moisture.

questions.question_text: For a chemical reaction R → P, the variation in the concentration [R] vs time t plot is given as: [Image: linear decreasing plot]. (a) Predict the order of the reaction and write the unit of rate constant (k) for this order of reaction. (b) What is the slope of the curve?
questions.correct_answer: (a) Zero order reaction. Unit of k = mol L⁻¹ s⁻¹. (b) Slope = -k.
questions.solution_summary: A plot of [R] vs t is a straight line with negative slope for a zero order reaction. The rate law is [R] = [R]₀ - kt, so slope = -k.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Interpretation of concentration vs time graph for zero order reaction.

questions.question_text: Write the chemical equations when: (a) Ethanal is treated with 2,4-dinitrophenylhydrazine? (b) Propanone is treated with Zn(Hg) and conc. HCl?
questions.topic: Organic Chemistry - Aldehydes, Ketones and Carboxylic Acids
questions.sub_topic: Tests and Reactions of Carbonyl Compounds
questions.correct_answer: (a) Ethanal + 2,4-DNP → 2,4-dinitrophenylhydrazone of ethanal (orange/yellow precipitate). (b) Propanone + Zn(Hg)/HCl → propane (Clemmensen reduction).
questions.solution_summary: Aldehydes/ketones react with 2,4-DNP to form hydrazones (used for identification). Clemmensen reduction of ketones to alkanes.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Recall of 2,4-DNP test and Clemmensen reduction.

questions.question_text: Calculate the potential of Iron electrode in which the concentration of Fe2+ ion is 0.01 M. (E°Fe2+/Fe = -0.45 V at 298 K) [Given: log 10 = 1]
questions.solution_summary: Apply Nernst equation for Fe2+/Fe electrode.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Application of Nernst equation.

questions.question_text: The following initial rate data were obtained for the reaction: 2NO(g) + Br2(g) → 2NOBr(g). [Table: Expt 1: [NO]=0.05, [Br2]=0.05, Rate=1.0×10^-3; Expt 2: [NO]=0.05, [Br2]=0.15, Rate=3.0×10^-3; Expt 3: [NO]=0.15, [Br2]=0.05, Rate=9.0×10^-3]. (a) What is the order with respect to NO and Br2? (b) Calculate the rate constant (k). (c) Determine the rate of reaction when [NO]=0.4M and [Br2]=0.2M.
questions.sub_topic: Determination of Order from Initial Rate Data
questions.correct_answer: (a) Order w.r.t NO = 2, order w.r.t Br2 = 1. (b) k = 8 L² mol⁻² s⁻¹. (c) Rate = 0.256 mol L⁻¹ s⁻¹.
questions.solution_summary: Using initial rate data: when [Br2] triples (keeping [NO] constant), rate triples → order 1 for Br2. When [NO] triples (keeping [Br2] constant), rate increases 9 times → order 2 for NO. Rate law: Rate = k[NO]^2[Br2]. Calculate k from experiment 1. Then substitute for given concentrations.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Analysis of initial rate data and application of rate law.

questions.question_text: When a certain conductivity cell was filled with 0.05 M KCl solution, it has a resistance of 100 ohm at 25°C. When the same cell was filled with 0.02 M AgNO3 solution, the resistance was 90 ohm. Calculate the conductivity and molar conductivity of AgNO3 solution. (Given: Conductivity of 0.05 M KCl solution = 1.35 × 10^-2 ohm^-1 cm^-1)
questions.solution_summary: Use cell constant from KCl measurement, then calculate conductivity and molar conductivity for AgNO3.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Application of cell constant and conductivity formulas.

questions.question_text: (a) Which of the following is an allylic halide? (i) CH3-CH=CH-Br (ii) CH2=CH-CH(Br)-CH3. (b) Out of chlorobenzene and 2,4,6-trinitrochlorobenzene, which is more reactive towards nucleophilic substitution and why? (c) Which isomer of C4H9Cl has the lowest boiling point?
questions.correct_answer: (a) (ii) is allylic halide (Br attached to carbon adjacent to C=C). (b) 2,4,6-Trinitrochlorobenzene is more reactive because nitro groups are electron withdrawing, stabilising the carbanion intermediate in SNAr. (c) tert-butyl chloride (2-chloro-2-methylpropane) has the lowest boiling point due to maximum branching.
questions.solution_summary: Identification of allylic halide, effect of substituents on SNAr reactivity, and effect of branching on boiling point.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Understanding of allylic halides, electron-withdrawing groups in SNAr, and factors affecting boiling points.

questions.question_text: (a) Write the formula for the following coordination compound: Potassium tetrahydroxidozinate (II). (b) Arrange the following complexes in the increasing order of conductivity of their solution: [Cr(NH3)5Cl]Cl2, [Cr(NH3)3Cl3], [Cr(NH3)6]Cl3. (c) Identify the type of isomerism exhibited by the following complexes: (i) [Co(NH3)5NO2]^2+ (ii) [Co(en)3]Cl3
questions.correct_answer: (a) K2[Zn(OH)4]. (b) [Cr(NH3)3Cl3] (neutral) < [Cr(NH3)5Cl]Cl2 (2 ions) < [Cr(NH3)6]Cl3 (3 ions). (c)(i) Linkage isomerism (NO2 vs ONO), (ii) Optical isomerism.
questions.solution_summary: Formula writing, conductivity order based on number of ions produced, and identification of isomerism types.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Application of IUPAC rules, conductivity based on ionisation, and isomerism identification.

questions.question_text: Compound (A) (C6H12O2) on reduction with LiAlH4 gives two compounds (B) and (C). The compound (B) on oxidation with PCC gives compound (D) which upon treatment with dilute NaOH and subsequent heating gives compound (E). Compound (E) on catalytic hydrogenation gives compound (C). The compound (D) is oxidized further to give compound (F) which is found to be a monobasic acid (Molecular weight = 60). Identify the compounds (A), (B), (C), (D), (E) and (F).
questions.topic: Organic Chemistry - Aldehydes, Ketones and Carboxylic Acids
questions.correct_answer: A = CH3COOC2H5 (ethyl acetate). B = C2H5OH (ethanol). C = CH3OH (methanol). D = CH3CHO (acetaldehyde). E = CH3CH=CHCHO (crotonaldehyde). F = CH3COOH (acetic acid, MW=60).
questions.solution_summary: Stepwise identification: ester hydrolysis/reduction gives ethanol and methanol. Ethanol to acetaldehyde, aldol condensation to crotonaldehyde, hydrogenation to methanol, and oxidation of acetaldehyde to acetic acid.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Connecting multiple reactions to deduce structures.

questions.question_text: (a) Write the mechanism of the following reaction: CH3CH2OH →[H+] CH2=CH2. (b) Write the main product in each of the following reactions: (i) CH3-CH2-CH=CH2 → a) B2H6 b) 3H2O2/OH- (ii) oxidation of ketone [Image]
questions.topic: Organic Chemistry - Alcohols, Phenols and Ethers / Aldehydes, Ketones
questions.correct_answer: (a) Mechanism: Protonation of OH, loss of water to form carbocation (rate determining), deprotonation to form ethene. (b)(i) Hydroboration-oxidation of but-1-ene gives butan-1-ol (anti-Markovnikov). (ii) Oxidation of ketone gives carboxylic acids (cleavage).
questions.solution_summary: Acid-catalysed dehydration proceeds via carbocation; hydroboration-oxidation gives anti-Markovnikov alcohol; ketone oxidation cleaves to acids.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Mechanistic steps of dehydration, and products of hydroboration-oxidation and ketone oxidation.

questions.question_text: Answer the following: (any three) (a) What is peptide linkage? (b) What type of bonds hold a DNA double helix together? (c) Which one of the following is a polysaccharide? Sucrose, Glucose, Starch, Fructose. (d) Give one example each for water-soluble vitamins and fat-soluble vitamins.
questions.correct_answer: (a) Peptide linkage: -CO-NH- bond between amino acids. (b) Hydrogen bonds. (c) Starch. (d) Water-soluble: Vitamin C (or B-complex). Fat-soluble: Vitamin A (or D, E, K).
questions.solution_summary: Definitions and identification of biomolecules.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Recall of basic definitions and classification.

questions.question_text: Case: Batteries and Fuel Cells. (a) How do primary batteries differ from secondary batteries? (b) The cell potential of Mercury cell is 1.35 V, and remains constant during its life. Give reason. (c) Write the reactions involved in the recharging of the lead storage battery. OR (c) Write two advantages of fuel cells over other galvanic cells.
questions.correct_answer: (a) Primary batteries are not rechargeable; secondary batteries are rechargeable. (b) Overall reaction in mercury cell does not involve any change in ion concentration, so potential remains constant. (c) Recharging of lead storage battery: Cathode: PbSO4 + 2e- → Pb + SO4^2-; Anode: PbSO4 + 2H2O → PbO2 + SO4^2- + 4H+ + 2e-. OR (c) Advantages: higher efficiency (about 70%) and pollution-free (only water as by-product).
questions.solution_summary: Differences, reason for constant potential, and reactions/advantages.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Knowledge of battery types, mercury cell chemistry, and fuel cell advantages.

questions.question_text: Case: Crystal Field Theory. (a) What is crystal field splitting energy? (b) Give reason for the violet colour of the complex [Ti(H2O)6]^3+ on the basis of crystal field theory. (c) [Cr(NH3)6]^3+ is paramagnetic while [Ni(CN)4]^2- is diamagnetic. Explain why. [Atomic No.: Cr=24, Ni=28] OR (c) Explain why [Fe(CN)6]^3- is an inner orbital complex, whereas [Fe(H2O)6]^3+ is an outer orbital complex. [Atomic No.: Fe=26]
questions.correct_answer: (a) Crystal field splitting energy is the energy difference between t2g and eg orbitals in an octahedral field. (b) Ti3+ is d1; d-d transition from t2g to eg absorbs light, giving violet colour. (c) Cr3+ is d3, paramagnetic. Ni2+ in [Ni(CN)4]^2- is d8, CN- strong field, all electrons paired → diamagnetic. OR (c) CN- is strong field, causes pairing → d2sp3 (inner orbital). H2O weak field → sp3d2 (outer orbital).
questions.solution_summary: CFT definitions, explanation of colour, and magnetic/inner-outer orbital classification.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Deep understanding of CFT, d-d transitions, and magnetic properties.

questions.question_text: Answer any five of the following: (a) N,N-diethyl-benzenesulphonamide is insoluble in alkali. Give reason. (b) Aniline does not undergo Friedel-Crafts reaction. Why? (c) Write a simple chemical test to distinguish between methylamine and aniline. (d) Write the chemical reaction involved in Gabriel phthalimide synthesis. (e) How will you convert aniline to p-bromoaniline? (f) Complete the following reaction: [Image: Diazonium salt reaction]. (g) Write the structures of A and B in the following reaction: COOH + NH3 → A →[Br2+NaOH] B.
questions.correct_answer: (a) No H atom on N, so not acidic. (b) Aniline forms salt with AlCl3, deactivating the ring. (c) Carbylamine test: methylamine gives foul smell; aniline gives dye test. (d) Phthalimide + KOH → potassium phthalimide + RX → N-alkylphthalimide + hydrazine → primary amine. (e) Aniline + Br2 in acetic acid → p-bromoaniline. (f) Diazonium salt + phenol → azo dye. (g) A = benzamide, B = aniline (Hofmann degradation).
questions.solution_summary: Reasons, tests, and conversions involving amines.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Comprehensive understanding of amine chemistry, tests, and conversions.

questions.question_text: (a) (i) Account for the following: (1) The melting and boiling points of Zn, Cd and Hg are low. (2) Of the d4 species, Cr2+ is strongly reducing while Mn3+ is strongly oxidizing. (3) E° value of Cu2+/Cu is +0.34 V. (ii) Complete and balance: KMnO4 →[heat]; Cr2O7^2- + 6I^- + 14H+ →. OR (b) (i) Out of Cu2Cl2 and CuCl2, which is more stable in aqueous solution and why? (ii) Write the general electronic configuration of f-block elements. (iii) Predict which of the following will be coloured in aqueous solution and why? Sc3+, Fe3+, Zn2+. [Atomic numbers: Sc=21, Fe=26, Zn=30] (iv) How can you obtain potassium dichromate from sodium chromate? (v) Why do transition metals and their compounds show catalytic activities?
questions.topic: Inorganic Chemistry - d- and f-Block Elements
questions.correct_answer: (i)(1) Zn, Cd, Hg have fully filled d10, weak metallic bonding. (2) Cr2+ → Cr3+ (stable d3); Mn3+ → Mn2+ (stable d5). (3) Cu has high atomisation enthalpy and low hydration enthalpy. (ii) 2KMnO4 → K2MnO4 + MnO2 + O2; Cr2O7^2- + 6I^- + 14H+ → 2Cr3+ + 3I2 + 7H2O. OR (b)(i) CuCl2 is more stable (Cu2+ has high hydration enthalpy). (ii) (n-2)f^1-14 (n-1)d^0-1 ns^2. (iii) Fe3+ is coloured (d5, unpaired), Sc3+ (d0) and Zn2+ (d10) are colourless. (iv) 2Na2CrO4 + H2SO4 → Na2Cr2O7; Na2Cr2O7 + 2KCl → K2Cr2O7 + 2NaCl. (v) Variable oxidation states and ability to form complexes.
questions.solution_summary: Reasons for periodic trends, redox behaviour, equations, and transition metal properties.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Deep understanding of d-block chemistry, redox behaviour, and catalytic properties.

questions.question_text: (a) (i) At the same temperature, CO2 gas is more soluble in water than O2 gas. Which one of them will have higher value of KH and why? (ii) How does the size of blood cells change when placed in an aqueous solution containing more than 0.9% (mass/volume) sodium chloride? (iii) 1 molal aqueous solution of an electrolyte A2B3 is 60% ionized. Calculate the boiling point of the solution. (Given: Kb for H2O = 0.52 K kg mol^-1) OR (b) (i) The vapour pressures of A and B at 25°C are 75 mm Hg and 25 mm Hg, respectively. If A and B are mixed such that the mole fraction of A in the mixture is 0.4, then calculate the mole fraction of B in vapour phase. (ii) Define colligative property. Which colligative property is preferred for the molar mass determination of macromolecules? (iii) Why are equimolar solutions of sodium chloride and glucose not isotonic?
questions.correct_answer: (a)(i) O2 has higher KH because KH is inversely proportional to solubility. (ii) Blood cells shrink (crenation) due to osmosis. (iii) A2B3 → 2A^3+ + 3B^2-, n=5, i=1+(n-1)α = 1+4×0.6=3.4. ΔTb = i × Kb × m = 3.4 × 0.52 × 1 = 1.768 K, Tb = 374.768 K. OR (b)(i) PT = 75×0.4 + 25×0.6 = 45 mm Hg, yB = (25×0.6)/45 = 1/3. (ii) Colligative property depends on number of solute particles; osmotic pressure is preferred for macromolecules. (iii) NaCl dissociates (i=2), glucose does not (i=1), so they have different osmotic pressures.
questions.solution_summary: Application of Henry's law, osmosis, colligative properties, Raoult's law, and van't Hoff factor.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Multi-concept application: Henry's law, osmosis, boiling point elevation, Raoult's law, and colligative properties.
